DEPGTY 
The Mayor and two Councilmen of the City of Burbank are 
among the organizers of a local bank. 
They will be on the board 
of directors and will each invest at least $1,000 in shares of 
the bank. 
We would appreciate an advice letter as to whether there 
would be a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act 
should funds of the City be deposited in the bank. 
Under the City Charter, the City Treasurer, an elected 
official, is responsible for the custody of all City funds. 
Section 53635 of the Government Code confers on the treasurer 
of a local agency the power to select the state or national 
bank or banks where funds belonging to, or in the custody of, 
the local agency are to be deposited. 
Even if said deposits were considered investments, the City 
Council has, pursuant to Section 53635 of the Government Code, 
delegated to the City Treasurer the authority to invest City 
funds. 
Such authority may not be controlled once it is delegated. 
In view of the foregoing, as long as the ~1ayor and two Council-
men refrain from in any way attempting to use their official posi-
tion to influence the City Treasurer, it would appear that there 
will be no conflict of interest should funds of the City be de-
posited in the bank in question. 
275 E, OLIVE AVE .• P.O, BOX 6459 • 
BURBANK, CALIFORNIA 91510 


Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr. 
Chief, Conflicts of Interest Division 
August 22, 1977 
Page 2 
While not relevant, it is noteworthy that such deposit 
would not violate Section 1090 of the Government Code. 
(See 
Section 860 of the Financial Code, which states that a bank 
may act as a depository for the handling of public funds not-
withstanding the fact that a member of the legislative body or 
an officer or employee of the depositor is an official, employee 
or stockholder of such bank.) 
We would appreciate an advice letter as to whether there 
would be a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act 
provided there is no influence exercised. 
A copy of our opinion on the City Treasurer's duties and 
responsibilities is enclosed.

OFFICE OF CITY ATTORNEY 
DATE: 
July 8, 1969 
OPINION NO. 69-17 
TO: 
,Hr. Joseph N. Baker, Ci tj[ l-1anager 
SUBJECT: 
City Treasurer -
Duties and Responsibilities 
By your memorandum dated June II, 1969 you asked 
our opinion as to the feasibility of a dual signature procedure 
for wi thdra~val of City funds in lieu of increasing the City 
Treasurer1s bond and whether or not the City Manager has the 
right to set up procedures which would act as a- control factor 
over th~ City Treasurer. 
Obstacles to the implementation or adoption of a 
dual signature procedure for \vi thdrawal of City funds from 
bank accounts are found not only in the City Charter but also 
in the Government Code. 
Section 13 of the Charter provides as follows with 
respect to the City Treasurer: 
"There shall be a City Treasurer elected every 
four years-at the general municipal election. It 
shall be his duty to receive and safely keep all 
moneys and securities belonging to the city and com-
ing into his hands, and payout: the same only on 
warrants signed by the proper officers and not other-
\vise. 
He shall hold office for four years and until 
his successor is elected and qualified.~ 
Since under this section the duty is imposed on the 
City Treasurer to "safely keep all moneys and securities belong":' 
ing to the city" any attempt to restrict his ability to shift 
funds from one bank to another or to withdraw the funds, by re-' 
quiring an additional signature, might be held to be violative 
of the Charter provision. 
An ordinance can no more change or 
limit the effect of the charter than a statute can modify or 
supersede a provision of t.11e State Constitution {J"larculescu v. 
City Planning Corrmission, 7 Cal.App.2d 371, 46 P.2d 308, 309~ 
A pertinent example.of the application of this rule is found in 
Hartford Accident £ Indemnity Co. ~ City of Tulare, 30 Cal.2d 
832, 186 P .2d 121, \'lhere the Supreme Court held a city ordinance 
void where it purported to authorize a $10,000.00 bond for the 
City Treasurer when the charter prescribed a $50,000.00 bond 


£·lr. Joseph N. Baker 
Ci ty ,r.~anager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17 
Page 2 
for that office. 
In' terms of Constitutional offices the Attorney 
General has ruled on several occasions that when the Constitution 
declares that certain pow'ers be possessed and certain duties 'per-
formed by a particular officer or board, no other officer, de-
partment or board may exercise and discharge those pm'lers and 
duties (See 32 9~ Atty. Gen. 55), and any attempt by the Legis-
lature to interfere with the discretion inherent in the exercise 
of such powers and duties by requiring a further approval by 
some other officer, is a nullity (See Qps. Atty. Gen. No. NS-584 
and No. NS-584a). 
The charter of a municipal corporation is its 
constitution (In re Pfahler, 150 Cal. 71, 88 Pac. 270). 
With 
respect to bank-accounts in which City funds are deposited, it is 
possible to conclude under our Charter provision, as the Supreme 
Court of Oklahoma did in Ferrell ~ Tmvn of l'-10untain View (Okla.) 
260 Pac. 470, 471, that: 
"In our opinion, a tmvn treasurer is the proper 
custodian of the town's funds and he is an officer who 
acts upon his own iesponsibility and independently of 
t.'1e board of trustees of the tmvn, so far as the keep-
ing of the funds of the tmvn is concerned. 
He have 
examined the provisions of the statutes relative to 
the pm'lers and duties of the board of trustees I but 
none of them give the board power to direct the treasurer 
where, or in what manner, the funds shall be kept or 
invested. 
If the board could make an imperative order 
on him to invest them in certificates of deposit of a 
certain bank, it could make an order that he keep them 
in a private safe or place them elsewhere, as the fancy 
of the board might dictate, regardless of.their safety. 
If the board could require him to invest said funds in 
certain certificates of deposit, and therehy take from 
him the necessity of exercising his m-ln discretion and 
prudence in the matter, and relieve him of such respon-
sibility, it might require him to deliver said funds 
to the board, vlhich would become custodian thereof, al-
though'the members of said board are not required by 
statute to give a bond." 
In connection with t.'1e foregoing discussion the follmving addi-
tional decisions fxom other jurisdictions are pertinent and sup-
portive; 
r'Jalton v. Donnelly, 83 Okla. 233, 201 Pac. 367; ~'Jright 
v. Callahan, 
61 Ida. ~67, 99 P.2d 961; HcNicho~ v. C~ty ~ 
COunty of Denver (Colo), 230 P.2d 591, and Smylle v. Wl11iams, 
(Idaho) 341 P.2d 451. 


I·:r. Joseph N. Baker 
Ci ty ,Hanager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17 
Page 3 
As to the ability of the City Manager to set up procedures 
which would act as a control factor!over the City Treasurer, it 
should be noted that there is nothing in our Charter which confers 
on any officer, including the City Manager, the Hayor and the 
Council, any right to directly control the actions of the City 
Treasurer or the manner in which he shall perform his duties. 
\'Jhile the City Jv:anager is declared to be the head' of the admin-
istrative branch of the City government, any power to remove 
elected 'officers qr to fi~ their compensation is specifically 
excluded (Sections 10 (b) 3 and 24). 
t'i'hile the Council may assign 
the Ci ty £.lanager addi tiona 1 duties, it may not do so if such 
duties are "inconsistent" with the Charter (Section 10(b)7). 
The other Charter provisions relative to the City Treasurer re-
inforce, to a degree, his independent status in the performance 
of his duties. 
Section 45 requires that all moneys collected 
for the City by an~ officer or department be paid into the 
treasury daily. 
Under Section 20 the Council is empm ..... ered to 
fix by ordinance the amount of the City Treasurer's bond. 
Under Section 50, the ,Hayor, City Clerk and City Manager are· 
required to count the money and other securities in the treasury 
at least once every three months to- ascertain if the amounts 
on hand tally with the amounts that should be in the treasury 
according to, the ~ooks of the City. 
Section 52 requires the 
Council to employ a certified public accountant to investigate 
.. annually or oftener L~e transactions and accounts of all officers 
or employees having the collection, custody or disbursement of 
public money or property.· The import of these seve~al sections 
of the Charter is that the responsibility for safeguarding City 
funds and securities is vested in a bonded elected City officer 
free from interference "as to how he shall discharge this respon-
sibility but sUbj'ect· to periodic audit to insure.that no breach 
of trust has occurred.-. 
Even if this construction of the City Charter is 
erroneous or not warranted, the provisions of Sections 53630-53681 
of the Goverr~,ent Code relating to the deposit of funds by a local 
agency would apparently prevent the City Manager or the Council 
from imposing conditions on the ability of the City Treasurer to 
transfer City funds from one bank to another or to withdraw such 
funds. 
'l'his appears to be so even though it was recognized in an 
early decision of the California Supreme Court that the deposit of 
the funds of a charter city was a "municipal affair" (Rothschild 
v. Bantel, 152 Cal. 5, 91 Pac. 803, 805). 
To demonstrate the va-
l:[dity of this conclusion requires a consideration of Section 
16 1/2 of Article· 11 of the California Constitution and the sections 
of the Government Code referred to above which were adopted pursuant 
to the authority of this constitutional provision. 
Until the adop-
• 


Hr. Joseph N. Baker 
Ci ty IvIanager 
July 8, 1969 
( 
Opinion No. 69-17 
Page 4 
tion of this particular section of the Constitution in 1906 the 
deposit of municipal funds in banks was prohibited and any 
Charter provision which authorized such deposits was void 
(Yarnell ~ City of Los Angeles, 87 Cal. 603, 25 Pac. 767). 
As originally adopted this constitutional provision authorized 
municipalities to deposit their funds in national banks or banks 
organized t::.nder the laws of California "in such manner and under 
such conditions as may be provided by law" if certain require-
ments were met by the banks in which such funds were deposited 
and no more than 20% of the available ·funds were deposited in 
anyone bank while there were other banks requesting such de-
posi ts. 
The words 
II as may be provided by lavl" were construed in 
Rothschild v. Bantel, supra, as including a city charter with the 
result that-a city by its charter, and possibly by ordinance, 
could still prohibit such deposits or prescribe conditions of 
deposit differing from those prescribed by State law. 
However, 
in 1922 Section 16 1/2 of Article 11 was amended by adding on 
the phrase quoted above the following: 
II adopted by the people 
under the initiative or by a two-thirds vote of each house of the 
legislature and approved by the governor • .• 
11 and making other 
changes not pertinent to ti1is discussion. 
The sections of the 
Government Code above referred to 'were adopted by a tHo-thirds vote 
of each house of the legislature and approved by the governor. 
In XcGuire v. \'lent\V'orth, 120 Cal.A}?p. 340, 7 P.2d 729, the ques-' 
tion before the court v,las whether, a State statute similarly adopted 
pursuant to Dlat Constitutional provision prevailed over the pro-
visions of the Charter of the City and County of San Francisco as 
to the deposit of public funds. 
In holding that it did the Court 
stated as follows [7 P.2d 7302: 
"Both on the reasoning of 'the Suprerne .court in the 
Rothschild Case and under gen~ral principles -G~e statute 
of 1927 adopted by the required two-thirds vote of each . 
House of the Legislature must nm-l be held to be the cori-
trolling law upon the subject in cha.rter cities, regard-
less of previously existing or subsequently adopted 
charter provisions in conflict therewith. 
This is so 
because section 16 1/2, article 11, as nOvl amended, not 
only constitutes the sole authority for depositing public 
moneys, but limits the exercise of that authority to the 
manner and conditions provided by such laHs as may be 
adopted in one of the specific modes therein set fort~. 
This being a specific constitutional enactment on a spe-
cific subject must control the general constitutional 
provision on municipal affairs in so far as the two 
conflict." 


Nr. Joseph N. Baker 
Ci ty I':anager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17 
Page 5 
It \'lould therefore app~ar that the City is powerless 
to amend its Charter or enact other legislation as to the deposit 
of City funds and ~~at in this area of activity it is controlled 
by the sections of the Government Code relating to that subject. 
~'Je will now consider th.ose provisions insofar as they pertain to 
the question under discussion. 
Section 53633 provides that the 
treasurer shall determine the amount of money to be deposited 
as inactive and active deposits. 
Under Section 53634 the treas-
urer may call in money from inactive deposits and place it in 
active deposits as current demands require. 
Section 53635 con-
fers on the treasurer the power to select the state or national 
l~anks vlhere the money belonging to,' or in the custody of, a 
local agency is to be deposited. 
Section 53642 provides that 
the money deposited may be dra,·m out by check or order of the 
treasurer or other official authorized to make such deposit. 
Sections 53655, 53659, 53660 and 53661 confer on the treasurer 
certain discretionary power and authority with respect to securi-
ties deposited with him by participating banks. 
The total cioncept of this legislation is that the treas~ 
urer shall control all aspects of the deposit of public funds 
\'lith banks, without interference or restraint. 
This has not 
always been the case. 
For example, in a general statute passed 
by the Legislature in 1927 (Stats. 1927, p. 1388) the treasurer 
was required in most of these situations to obtain the consent 
of the governing body of the local ~gency or the approval of the 
attorney for such agency. 
The fact-that any such consent or ap-
proval is no longer required under the present provisions of the 
Government Code adds strength to the conclusion that no restraints 
or restrictions may be placed on the City Treasurer in his hand-
ling and management of City funds deposited in bgnks (See also 
8 Ops.Atty.Gen. 39). 
The foregoing conclusions would remain valid even if 
the office of City Treasurer were changed from an elective to 
an appointive office. 
We should point out that the investment of public funds 
is an entirely different matter and, by virtue of Section 60 of 
our Charter, is governed by Sections 53600-53608 of the Govern-
ment Code. 
Under these_sections it is the legislative body on 
whom the authority to make investments is conferred, not the 
treasurer. 
However, under the provisions of Section 53607 this 
authority may be delegated to the treasurer. 
Section 53608 


Fir. Joseph N. Baker 
City Nanager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17 
Page 6 
authorizes the legislative body to geposit for safekeeping with 
a state or national bank within this State or with the Federal 
Reserve Bank. of San Francisco or any.branch thereof within this 
State, the bonas, notes, bills, etc., in which the money of the 
local agency is invested. 
Again, the authority.to do this may 
be delegated to the treasurer. 
If the power to invest is dele-
gated to the treasurer he assumes "full responsibility for such 
transactions until such time as the delegation of authority is 
revoked" and is re,quired to "make a monthly report of such trans-
actions to the legislative body" (Section 53607). 
In view of 
tilis provision it is doubtful that any conditions or restrictions 
could be imposed on the exercise of such authority 
by the City 
Treasurer. 
Hmvever, the Council could require that the securities 
be deposited in a bank in accordance with the provisions of Sec-
tion 53608, sUbject to withdrawal only by certain officers desig-
nated by it. In otoher vlOrds a dual signature requirement could 
be imposed on the withdrawal of securities from such depositories. 
It must be borne in minq., however, that where funds or securities. 
are removed from the control of the City Treasurer, neither he 
nor his sureties will be liable if such funds or securities are 
lost or misappropriated (See City oi vloodland ~ Leech, 20 Cal.App. 
IS, 127 Pac. ~040) • 
CONCLUSION 
In the final analysis the only means available under pre-
sent la,,,, of insuring the safety of money and securities under b'le 
control of the City Treasurer is a surety bond adequate in amount. 
~\lhat b'le principa;L .sum ·of such a bond should be deserves careful 
st.udy. 
One of the· .factors to be considered is whether under 
existing banking p-ractit;es the City Treasurer or"his.deputy could 
achieve a transfer of City funds in any substantial amount to his 
own account or convert, amounts on deposit or certificates of de-
posit to cash. 
In such a determination the amount of securities 
negotiable by delivery must be taken into account, bearing in mind 
~lat even though the Council assumes control of the securities in 
T,'lhich public funds are invested, th~ City Treasurer will still 
have exclusive control of securities deposited with him by banks 
in Hhich City accounts are maintained, as required by Section 
53651 of the Government Code. 
In this connection it appears to 
be the rule, supported by the overwhelming weight of authority, 
tllat the mere fact that a fiduciary transfers trust funds into 
his personal account will not of itself charge the bank with 
knowledge or notice that he is misappropriating or will misappro-
• 


Mr. Joseph N. Baker 
Ci ty j\1anager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17 
Page 7 
priate such funds (145 A.L.R. 445). 
\\'hen all or a portion of 
public funds are used to pay the individual debt of a public 
officer to the bank where such funds are deposited, notice of 
their character has been imputed in some cases. 
(See Gilliland 
v. Lincoln-Alliance Bank & T. Co., 239 Anp.Div. 68, 264 N.Y.S. 
779, aff'd 264 N.Y. 517, 191 N-:E. 543; !1~ryland Casualty Co. 
~ City N~~. Bank, 29 F.2d 662, 145 A.L.R. 445). 
There are 
also some decisions which hold the bank liable\ on the theory 
that it \\7as charged with notice that public funds may be deposi-
ted only in designated depositories (See Employers' Liability 
Assur. Corp. ~ Hudson River Trust Co., 250 App.Div. 159, 812, 
294 N.Y.S. 698; Fidelity £ C. Co. v. Farmers Nat. Bank, 275 N.Y. 
194, 9'N.E.2d 833). 
The same considerations which motivated the inquiry ,as 
to whether the $50,000.00 bond requirement for the City Treasurer 
was sufficient under present conditions, merit a further inquiry 
into the protection \vhich the City nOH has against misappropria-
tion of City funds by other personnel of the City Treasurer's 
office. 
The present bond of the City Treasurer is conditioned 
,on his faithful performance of the duties of that office. 
No 
reference is made to the Assistant City Treasurer or other posi-
tions in that office. 
There was a time in the statutory history 
of the State ,,,,hen the sureties on the official' bond of a public . 
officer were liable for wrongful acts or omissions of his deputies 
and other employees in his office even though no mention was made 
of such deputies or employees in the public officer's bond. (See 
5 ops. At~.Gen. 114; Union Bank and Trust Co. ~ Los Angeles, 
11 Cal.2d 675, 81 P.2d 919). 
However, in 1949, the Legislature 
added Section 1953.5 to the Government Code, and as further 
amended in 1951, provideo.: 
"No officer of the State, or of any 
aistrict, county, city, or judicial district is liable for moneys 
stolen from his official custody unless the loss was sustained 
because the officer failed to exercise due care." 
As a result 
the California Sunreme Court, in the case of County of Placer v. 
Aetna Casualty and Surety Co. (1958) I 
50 C.2d 182,"323 P.2d 753, 
held that a public officer and his sureties were no longer liable 
where rr,oney in his custody is embezzled by a clerk unless it is 
established that he failed to exerc~se due care. 
Although not 
referred to in the Court's opinion Section 1953.6 was also added 
to the Goverru~ent Code in 1951 and provided as folloHs: 
No officer of a county, city, or city and countYI 
\'1hose sole compensation by virtue of his office is a 
fixed salary established by the Legislature, the local 


" 
' 
r·lr. Joseph N. Baker 
City Manager 
Opinion No. 69-17 
p'age 8 
July 8, 1969 
f 
governing body, or b"le board of supervisors, shall be 
personally liable for the negligent act or omission of 
any deputy or employee serving under him anq performing 
the duties of his office, where the appointment or quali-
fication of such deputy or employee is required to be 
and has been approved by the local governing body or the 
board of supervisors, or by the civil service commission, 
unless the officer failed to exercise due care in the 
selection, appointment, or supervision of such deputy 
or employee, or negligently failed to suspend or secure 
the discharge of such deputy or employee after knowledge 
or notice of his inefficiency or incompetency. 
Nothing in this section shall be interpreted as, 
placing any liability upon the principal officer for 
the act of a deputy or employee unless such liability 
is othenlise imposed upon the principal officer by law, 
nor shall this section be construed or interpreted as 
releasing or relieving any such county, city, or city 
and county of any liability for the negligent act or 
omission of any such deputy or employee otherwise im-
posed by law. 
Section 1504 of the Government Code, which \'las the foundation for 
the previous decisions, 't1as anended in 1955 to render that sec-
tion inoperative as a basis for imposing liability on a public 
officer or his sureties for the acts or omissions of his deputies 
or employees. 
At the time of the adoption of ~~e California Tort 
Liability Act in 1963, Sections 1953.5 and 1953.6 of the Government 
Code were repealed and replaced by Sections 820.8 and 822 which 
provide as follows: 
§820.8. 
. . 
Except as otherwise provi~ed by statute, a public 
en~ployee is not liable for an injury caused by the act 
or omission of ano~~er person. 
Nothing in this section 
exonerates a public employee from liability for injury 
proxiuately caused by his OVln negligent or 't'lrongful act 
or omission. 
§S22. 
A public er.lployee is not liable for money stolen 
from his official custody. 
Nothing in this section 
exonerates a public employee .from liability if the 
loss was sustained as a result of his own negligent 
or wrongful act or omission. 


Mr. Joseph N. Baker 
Ci ty £-lanager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17 
Page 9 
In the Legislative Corruaittee Comment appended to Section 
822 it is stated: 
This section makes clear that statutes which require 
a public employee to account for money in his official 
custody do not impose liability for a loss unless the 
loss is sustained as a result of his own negligent or 
wrongful act or omission. 
The section has no effect 
on liability based on a bond, since section 814 provides 
that this par·t does not affect liability based on contract. 
It thus appear~ that He can no longer rely on the City 
Treasurer's bond for the recovery of City funds which might be 
misappropriated by personnel in his office, except where the 
loss is sustained as a result of his negligent or wrongful act 
or omis sion.
o 
Lacking this the City would be protected only to 
the extent of $10.,000.00 (See Section 2-1101{a), Burbank Huni-
cipal Code.) 
We should point out t.."1at perhaps as great a danger, if 
not a greater danger, of "misappropriation of public funds exists 
among those, other than the City 'I'reasurer I \-Iho have the responsi-
. bili ty of signing. Ci ty. vlarrants. 
This would include the Finance 
Director and the,1:'.ccounting Officer (See Sec. 2-1203, Burbank I:1uni-
cipal Code). 
The bOnd provided for these positions is $10,000.00 
(Sec. 2-1101(a) , <Burbank Hunicipal Code). 
No additional protec-
tion is provided by the fact that warrants, before negotiability 
is achieved, must be ,also signed by the City Treasurer. 
The ob-
ligation of the City Treasurer is limited in this respect by Sec-
tion 13 of the Charter which requires that he payout City funds 
"only on warrants signed by t.~e proper officers._ II 
While his sig-
nature is necessary he is under no, duty to inquire further if the 
warrant bears the signature of the'Finance Director, or in his 
absence, the Accounting Officer. 
If a warrant is used by the 
other signatories as a means of misappropriating City funds, 
we could not look to the City Treasurer or his surety for relief, 
so long as the City Treasurer vIas without notice, actual or con-
structive, of any fraud or infirmity in the claim on which the 
\'Jarrant \'las based. 
(See Los Angeles County v. Lankershim, 100 
Cal. 525, 35 Pac. 153, 556). 
Consequently, although two signa-
tures are nmv required on City warrants, dual signature protec-
tion is not achieved. 
• 


z,lr. Joseph N. Baker 
City Hanager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17 
Page 10 
Consideration should also be given to requiring more 
sUbstantial bonds of those involved in issuing and processing 
warrants. 
The mechanization of the routine of is~uing warrants 
and the use of facsimile signatures creates a danger greater 
than previously existed when warrants were processed by hand. 
A City warrant issued to a named payee is far more easy to nego-
tiate than, for example, a certificate of deposit issued in the 
name of the City.

sections.question: We would appreciate an advice letter as to whether there would be a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act should funds of the City be deposited in the bank in question.
sections.conclusion:
In the final analysis the only means available under pre-
sent la,,,, of insuring the safety of money and securities under b'le 
control of the City Treasurer is a surety bond adequate in amount. 
~\lhat b'le principa;L .sum ·of such a bond should be deserves careful 
st.udy. 
One of the· .factors to be considered is whether under 
existing banking p-ractit;es the City Treasurer or"his.deputy could 
achieve a transfer of City funds in any substantial amount to his 
own account or convert, amounts on deposit or certificates of de-
posit to cash. 
In such a determination the amount of securities 
negotiable by delivery must be taken into account, bearing in mind 
~lat even though the Council assumes control of the securities in 
T,'lhich public funds are invested, th~ City Treasurer will still 
have exclusive control of securities deposited with him by banks 
in Hhich City accounts are maintained, as required by Section 
53651 of the Government Code. 
In this connection it appears to 
be the rule, supported by the overwhelming weight of authority, 
tllat the mere fact that a fiduciary transfers trust funds into 
his personal account will not of itself charge the bank with 
knowledge or notice that he is misappropriating or will misappro-

priate such funds (145 A.L.R. 445). 
\\'hen all or a portion of 
public funds are used to pay the individual debt of a public 
officer to the bank where such funds are deposited, notice of 
their character has been imputed in some cases. 
(See Gilliland 
v. Lincoln-Alliance Bank & T. Co., 239 Anp.Div. 68, 264 N.Y.S. 
779, aff'd 264 N.Y. 517, 191 N-:E. 543; !1~ryland Casualty Co. 
~ City N~~. Bank, 29 F.2d 662, 145 A.L.R. 445). 
There are 
also some decisions which hold the bank liable\ on the theory 
that it \\7as charged with notice that public funds may be deposi-
ted only in designated depositories (See Employers' Liability 
Assur. Corp. ~ Hudson River Trust Co., 250 App.Div. 159, 812, 
294 N.Y.S. 698; Fidelity £ C. Co. v. Farmers Nat. Bank, 275 N.Y. 
194, 9'N.E.2d 833). 
The same considerations which motivated the inquiry ,as 
to whether the $50,000.00 bond requirement for the City Treasurer 
was sufficient under present conditions, merit a further inquiry 
into the protection \vhich the City nOH has against misappropria-
tion of City funds by other personnel of the City Treasurer's 
office. 
The present bond of the City Treasurer is conditioned 
,on his faithful performance of the duties of that office. 
No 
reference is made to the Assistant City Treasurer or other posi-
tions in that office. 
There was a time in the statutory history 
of the State ,,,,hen the sureties on the official' bond of a public . 
officer were liable for wrongful acts or omissions of his deputies 
and other employees in his office even though no mention was made 
of such deputies or employees in the public officer's bond. (See 
5 ops. At~.Gen. 114; Union Bank and Trust Co. ~ Los Angeles, 
11 Cal.2d 675, 81 P.2d 919). 
However, in 1949, the Legislature 
added Section 1953.5 to the Government Code, and as further 
amended in 1951, provideo.: 
"No officer of the State, or of any 
aistrict, county, city, or judicial district is liable for moneys 
stolen from his official custody unless the loss was sustained 
because the officer failed to exercise due care." 
As a result 
the California Sunreme Court, in the case of County of Placer v. 
Aetna Casualty and Surety Co. (1958) I 
50 C.2d 182,"323 P.2d 753, 
held that a public officer and his sureties were no longer liable 
where rr,oney in his custody is embezzled by a clerk unless it is 
established that he failed to exerc~se due care. 
Although not 
referred to in the Court's opinion Section 1953.6 was also added 
to the Goverru~ent Code in 1951 and provided as folloHs: 
No officer of a county, city, or city and countYI 
\'1hose sole compensation by virtue of his office is a 
fixed salary established by the Legislature, the local 


" 
' 
r·lr. Joseph N. Baker 
City Manager 
Opinion No. 69-17 
p'age 8 
July 8, 1969 
f 
governing body, or b"le board of supervisors, shall be 
personally liable for the negligent act or omission of 
any deputy or employee serving under him anq performing 
the duties of his office, where the appointment or quali-
fication of such deputy or employee is required to be 
and has been approved by the local governing body or the 
board of supervisors, or by the civil service commission, 
unless the officer failed to exercise due care in the 
selection, appointment, or supervision of such deputy 
or employee, or negligently failed to suspend or secure 
the discharge of such deputy or employee after knowledge 
or notice of his inefficiency or incompetency. 
Nothing in this section shall be interpreted as, 
placing any liability upon the principal officer for 
the act of a deputy or employee unless such liability 
is othenlise imposed upon the principal officer by law, 
nor shall this section be construed or interpreted as 
releasing or relieving any such county, city, or city 
and county of any liability for the negligent act or 
omission of any such deputy or employee otherwise im-
posed by law. 
Section 1504 of the Government Code, which \'las the foundation for 
the previous decisions, 't1as anended in 1955 to render that sec-
tion inoperative as a basis for imposing liability on a public 
officer or his sureties for the acts or omissions of his deputies 
or employees. 
At the time of the adoption of ~~e California Tort 
Liability Act in 1963, Sections 1953.5 and 1953.6 of the Government 
Code were repealed and replaced by Sections 820.8 and 822 which 
provide as follows: 
§820.8. 
. . 
Except as otherwise provi~ed by statute, a public 
en~ployee is not liable for an injury caused by the act 
or omission of ano~~er person. 
Nothing in this section 
exonerates a public employee from liability for injury 
proxiuately caused by his OVln negligent or 't'lrongful act 
or omission. 
§S22. 
A public er.lployee is not liable for money stolen 
from his official custody. 
Nothing in this section 
exonerates a public employee .from liability if the 
loss was sustained as a result of his own negligent 
or wrongful act or omission. 


Mr. Joseph N. Baker 
Ci ty £-lanager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17  
In the Legislative Corruaittee Comment appended to Section 
822 it is stated: 
This section makes clear that statutes which require 
a public employee to account for money in his official 
custody do not impose liability for a loss unless the 
loss is sustained as a result of his own negligent or 
wrongful act or omission. 
The section has no effect 
on liability based on a bond, since section 814 provides 
that this par·t does not affect liability based on contract. 
It thus appear~ that He can no longer rely on the City 
Treasurer's bond for the recovery of City funds which might be 
misappropriated by personnel in his office, except where the 
loss is sustained as a result of his negligent or wrongful act 
or omis sion.
o 
Lacking this the City would be protected only to 
the extent of $10.,000.00 (See Section 2-1101{a), Burbank Huni-
cipal Code.) 
We should point out t.."1at perhaps as great a danger, if 
not a greater danger, of "misappropriation of public funds exists 
among those, other than the City 'I'reasurer I \-Iho have the responsi-
. bili ty of signing. Ci ty. vlarrants. 
This would include the Finance 
Director and the,1:'.ccounting Officer (See Sec. 2-1203, Burbank I:1uni-
cipal Code). 
The bOnd provided for these positions is $10,000.00 
(Sec. 2-1101(a) , <Burbank Hunicipal Code). 
No additional protec-
tion is provided by the fact that warrants, before negotiability 
is achieved, must be ,also signed by the City Treasurer. 
The ob-
ligation of the City Treasurer is limited in this respect by Sec-
tion 13 of the Charter which requires that he payout City funds 
"only on warrants signed by t.~e proper officers._ II 
While his sig-
nature is necessary he is under no, duty to inquire further if the 
warrant bears the signature of the'Finance Director, or in his 
absence, the Accounting Officer. 
If a warrant is used by the 
other signatories as a means of misappropriating City funds, 
we could not look to the City Treasurer or his surety for relief, 
so long as the City Treasurer vIas without notice, actual or con-
structive, of any fraud or infirmity in the claim on which the 
\'Jarrant \'las based. 
(See Los Angeles County v. Lankershim, 100 
Cal. 525, 35 Pac. 153, 556). 
Consequently, although two signa-
tures are nmv required on City warrants, dual signature protec-
tion is not achieved. 
• 


z,lr. Joseph N. Baker 
City Hanager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17  
Consideration should also be given to requiring more 
sUbstantial bonds of those involved in issuing and processing 
warrants. 
The mechanization of the routine of is~uing warrants 
and the use of facsimile signatures creates a danger greater 
than previously existed when warrants were processed by hand. 
A City warrant issued to a named payee is far more easy to nego-
tiate than, for example, a certificate of deposit issued in the 
name of the City.

_;Richard L.

yrssistant City Attorney
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a conflict of interest exists under the Political Reform Act if the City of Burbank deposits funds in a bank where the Mayor and two Councilmen are organizers, board members, and investors, given that the City Treasurer has delegated authority to invest City funds.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No conflict of interest exists under the Political Reform Act provided the Mayor and Councilmen do not attempt to use their official positions to influence the City Treasurer's fund deposit decisions, as the Treasurer's delegated investment authority cannot be controlled once delegated.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes incoming request from City Attorney (August 22, 1977) followed by supporting City Attorney Opinion No. 69-17 (July 8, 1969). The FPPC response itself is not fully present in the document - only the City Attorney's request and supporting materials are clearly visible. The conclusion provided appears to be from the FPPC's implied response based on context and the opening paragraph acknowledging receipt. Confidence reduced due to incomplete FPPC response letter in the image.

QUESTION: We would appreciate an advice letter as to whether there would be a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act should funds of the City be deposited in the bank in question.

CONCLUSION: In the final analysis the only means available under pre-
sent la,,,, of insuring the safety of money and securities under b'le 
control of the City Treasurer is a surety bond adequate in amount. 
~\lhat b'le principa;L .sum ·of such a bond should be deserves careful 
st.udy. 
One of the· .factors to be considered is whether under 
existing banking p-ractit;es the City Treasurer or"his.deputy could 
achieve a transfer of City funds in any substantial amount to his 
own account or convert, amounts on deposit or certificates of de-
posit to cash. 
In such a determination the amount of securities 
negotiable by delivery must be taken into account, bearing in mind 
~lat even though the Council assumes control of the securities in 
T,'lhich public funds are invested, th~ City Treasurer will still 
have exclusive control of securities deposited with him by banks 
in Hhich City accounts are maintained, as required by Section 
53651 of the Government Code. 
In this connection it appears to 
be the rule, supported by the overwhelming weight of authority, 
tllat the mere fact that a fiduciary transfers trust funds into 
his personal account will not of itself charge the bank with 
knowledge or notice that he is misappropriating or will misappro-
• 


Mr. Joseph N. Baker 
Ci ty j\1anager 
July 8, 1969 
Opinion No. 69-17  
priate such funds (145 A.L.R. 445). 
\\'hen all or a portion of 
public funds are used to pay the individual debt of a public 
officer to the bank where such funds are deposited, notice of 
their character has been imputed in some cases. 
(See Gilliland 
v. Lincoln-Alliance Bank & T. Co., 239 Anp.Div. 68, 264 N.Y.S. 
779, aff'd 264 N.Y. 517, 191 N-:E. 543; !1~ryland Casualty Co. 
~ City N~~. Bank, 29 F.2d 662, 145 A.L.R. 445).

also some decisions which hold the bank liable\ on the theory

ted only in designated depositories (See Employers' Liability

embedding.first_500_words: ., orIlla air Political Practices Commissioll, October 1 ~ , Mr. amuel Gorl Ci Attorney, Burbank 275 East Olive Post Off co Box 64 9 , Cal fornia 915 Dear Mr. Go j 'fh us, 0- the Po i forth I trus t I you have free to eonta t 95804 "', J K STREET SACRAMENTO, 95814 5901 C,,"flirt of lnter ... t 322M41 which Govern- 1 tre surer to s no conflie of Government as set ve to your e , ¢ August 22, 1977 Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr. Chief, Conflicts of Interest Division Fair Political Practices Commission P. O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804 Dear Mr. Spurlock: SAMUEL GORLICK / CITY ATTORNEY RiCHARD L 51 ,JR ON Y MA~ CK ALA;-.J KALKIN OAViD N RAKOV ASS MAR L. MERR1CK DEPGTY The Mayor and two Councilmen of the City of Burbank are among the organizers of a local bank. They will be on the board of directors and will each invest at least $1,000 in shares of the bank. We would appreciate an advice letter as to whether there would be a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act should funds of the City be deposited in the bank. Under the City Charter, the City Treasurer, an elected official, is responsible for the custody of all City funds. Section 53635 of the Government Code confers on the treasurer of a local agency the power to select the state or national bank or banks where funds belonging to, or in the custody of, the local agency are to be deposited. Even if said deposits were considered investments, the City Council has, pursuant to Section 53635 of the Government Code, delegated to the City Treasurer the authority to invest City funds. Such authority may not be controlled once it is delegated. In view of the foregoing, as long as the ~1ayor and two Council- men refrain from in any way attempting to use their official posi- tion to influence the City Treasurer, it would appear that there will be no conflict of interest should funds of the City be de- posited in the bank in question. 275 E, OLIVE AVE .• P.O, BOX 6459 • BURBANK, CALIFORNIA 91510 Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr. Chief, Conflicts of Interest Division August 22, 1977 Page 2 While not relevant, it is noteworthy that such deposit would not violate Section 1090 of the Government Code. (See Section 860 of the Financial Code, which states that a bank may act as a depository for the handling of public funds not- withstanding the fact that a member of the legislative body or an officer or employee of the depositor is an official, employee or stockholder of such bank.) We would appreciate an advice letter as to whether there would be a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act provided there is no influence exercised. A copy of our opinion on the City Treasurer's duties and responsibilities is enclosed. SG:mc Enclosure cc: Mayor and Members
embedding.summary: The City Attorney of Burbank requests FPPC advice on whether a conflict of interest would arise under the Political Reform Act if the City deposits funds in a bank where the Mayor and two Councilmen are organizers and investors. The FPPC advises that no conflict exists as long as these officials do not attempt to influence the City Treasurer's independent authority to select depositories.
